observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, Inventory Committee and Municipal Accountant implement and follow the prescribed guidelines and procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006, particularly the conduct of physical count of all PPE, the recognition of PPE items found at the station, and disposition for non-existing/ missing PPE items, if any, for the purpose of one-time cleansing of PPE account balances of the LGU. 

We also recommended that the Inventory Committee:

Prepare the (Physical Inventory Plan) PIP to be approved by the Municipal Mayor, and submit the same to the COA Audit Team,

Strictly follow the PIP to be able   to complete the inventory taking activities; and

Prepare and submit the RPCPPE within the prescribed period
Further, we recommended that the Property Custodian maintain and update the PCs on a regular basis.

Lastly, we recommended that the Property Custodian and Municipal Accountant:

Undertake collaborative procedures to ensure that all PPEs included in the RPCPPE are duly recorded in their respective records and that the PCs maintained by the Property Custodian and the PPELCs maintained by the Accounting Office are reconciled, and

Prepare the necessary report on the results of the reconciliation to the Municipal Mayor for his appropriate action on non-existing/ missing properties.
observations.reason:
The audit recommendations remain unimplemented as o this report writing.

These are reiterated in Part II, Audit Observation No. 1.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal   Treasurer:

Notify the payees concerned at least    one month before a check becomes stale; 

Upon presentation by the payees of the stale checks, physically cancel and replace checks in accordance with Section 59 (3) of MNGAS for LGUs; and

Prepare at yearend a complete Schedule of Unreleased Checks together with the Report of Checks Issued (RCI) and submit to the Municipal Accountant as her basis in drawing a journal entry voucher restoring the cash equivalent to the unreleased checks and recognizing the appropriate payable/liability accounts.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

 Cancel all stale checks and revert to the Cash in Bank Account and record the Accounts Payable account as prescribed n Section 61 of MNGAS for LGUS: and

Revert all unreleased checks to the Cash in Bank account and recognize appropriate liability accounts at the end of every year and reverse the entries made in the ensuing year.
observations.reason: The same audit observations were noted during the conduct of CY 2024 audit. Audit observations on the Cash and Cash Equivalent account are discussed in Part II, Audit Observation No. 3.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Engineer:

Conduct verification of prior years’ PPAs which are recorded in the CIP account; and

Collate the pertinent documents relative to the CIP accounts and provide Project Status Report as well as Certificate of Project Completion and Inspection and Acceptance Report to the Municipal Accountant as her basis in the transfer of completed PPAs under the CIP account to its appropriate asset accounts; 

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

Transfer completed projects to appropriate PPE account; and

Make the necessary adjustments relative to the current and prior years' unrecorded depreciation expenses and impairment losses on the completed projects
observations.reason: Some prior years unimplemented projects were not verified  no  documents were recovered to support the reclassification to appropriate PPE account.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Assessor expedite the processing of Certificate of Title of the untitled lots in order to establish absolute ownership and protect the legal interest of the Municipality.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure and establish lot ownership, in the name of the Municipality, before construction of any structures and land improvements.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace and reconcile the amounts recorded in the books with the source documents and effect adjustments, if necessary.
observations.reason:
No budget allocation for CY 2024


Construction of buildings was undertaken while the lot titling are  on process.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor together with the Municipal Engineer, MPDC and other concerned personnel ensure that the PPAs to be funded out of the 20% DF are well-planned, as well as procurement-and-implementation-ready such that the factors that may impede implementation, such as weather condition and accessibility of the site, shall be considered.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, MPDC and Municipal Engineer fast-track and monitor the implementation of the PPAs funded out of the 20% Development Fund both for current and prior years’ balances to achieve the purpose of the fund.

It is also recommended that appropriations of all PPAs which are no longer feasible and have not yet been implemented, be reverted back to the unappropriated surplus of the 20% DF for re-appropriation to other development projects pursuant to budgetary rules.
observations.reason: Audit observations on the utilization of the CY 2024 and prior years’ appropriation of the 20% DF are discussed in Part II, Audit Observation No. 9.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
Municipal Inventory Committee to prepare annually the RPCPPE and submit a copy to the Office of the Municipal Accountant and Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year.
Supply Officer to:
Prepare and maintain PCs per category of PPE; and
Forward to the Municipal Accountant copy of Acceptance and Inspection Reports and other pertinent documents for the recording of deliveries in the appropriate ledger cards and recognition in the books of accounts.
We also recommended that the Municipal Inventory Committee and the Municipal Accountant undertake collaborative procedures to ensure that all PPEs included in the RPCPPE are duly recorded in their respective records and that the PCs maintained by the Property Custodian and the PELCs maintained by the Accounting Office are reconciled.
We recommended that the Municipal Assessor together with the members of the Appraisal and Disposal Committee, conduct immediate appraisal and disposal of all unserviceable properties following the procedures set forth in COA Circular 92-386 dated October 20, 1992, to prevent further deterioration and loss of additional income.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant derecognize/ drop from the books of accounts all disposed unserviceable properties and recognize gain/ loss on disposal.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Conduct a thorough verification and tracing process for the long-outstanding balances in the “Due to NGAs” account and take appropriate actions to identify whether these balances are for reclassification or for return to its appropriate source agency; and
Exert effort to identify whether these balances were non-moving for ten (10) years or more from the last transaction recorded in the books to consider its dormancy. Initiate request for write-off if these balances are warranted as dormant in compliance with the procedures set by the Commission on Audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor expedite the implementation of projects, programs and activities funded by various national government agencies for the benefit of the intended beneficiaries.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Draw journal entries to transfer the Municipality’s 20% share from Fire Code Fees to the “Trust Liabilities” account; and
Make the necessary entries to correct the misclassification of the National ADAC Award received from the DILG amounting to P100,00.00 in the “Due to NGAs” account. Henceforth, monetary awards received from national government agencies without specific purpose should be classified as “Subsidy from National Government” and utilization of which will be subjected to the appropriate budgetary rules and regulations.

We commended the Municipal Mayor and all personnel concerned for continuously adhering to Section 1, Rule VII of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9679 to ensure that monthly contributions and loan repayments withheld during the year were fully remitted to HDMF.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace and verify the adjusted amount of P4,005.02 recorded under JV No. 03-0280 in the Due to Officers and Employees account, remit the amount or make necessary adjustments as needed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Carefully review the payrolls prepared by the Payroll Officer to ensure that salaries of Municipal officials and personnel are deducted with the correct amounts due for remittance to PhilHealth and ensure that these deductions tally with the Preliminary Employees Premium Remittance List (PEPRL).
Correct the erroneous reclassification of the over-remittance of PhilHealth contributions totaling P1,209.51.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to coordinate with PhilHealth in facilitating the reconciliation of records concerning the unrecorded remittances for October 2016, November 2016, May 2017 and September 2017.

We commended the management for successfully implementing twenty-three out of twenty-five projects funded by the 20% Development Fund.
Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor to oversee the implementation of the projects, establish a system for monitoring the progress of implementation, and ensure submission that regular progress reports are prepared to track the utilization of the 20% DF.
We also recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), through the BAC Secretariat in coordination with the Budget Office, initiate the bidding process for all projects identified herewith as unimplemented. Henceforth, initiate the bidding for all projects identified in the Annual Budget without delay, following the timelines outlined in the Annual Procurement Plan (APP).
Finally, we recommended that management and the implementing offices include PPAs in the AIP only those development projects that are well-planned and procurement and implementation ready.

Henceforth, we recommended that if management intends to utilize loan proceeds for development projects, these should be classified separately from the 20% Development Fund and be properly authorized through an approved Supplemental Budget.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the LDRRM Council ensure full utilization of the LDRRMF thru implementation of all approved PPAs incorporated in the LDRRMF Investment Plan during the year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the members of the GFPS Technical Working Group (TWG) and GAD Focal Person to:
Empower the GAD Focal Point System to enhance their knowledge and build their competencies on GAD-related laws and international commitments, gender mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender-responsive planning and budgeting, and GAD-related tools among others; and require the GAD Focal Point System to prepare the GAD Agenda in accordance with the mandate of 4.C.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.
Coordinate with the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Planning Development Coordinator in preparing the GAD Accomplishment Report.
Plan the Municipality's activities aimed at achieving women's empowerment and addressing gender equality, focusing on priority projects while considering the Municipality's limited resources in terms of funds, time, manpower, and facility availability.
Refrain from implementing non-GAD-related activities and coordinate with the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that expenses excluded from the GPB and those not clearly addressing a specific gender issue are not charged to the GAD Budget.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer to:
Prepare the RAO for the GAD Budget.
Refrain from charging non-GAD-related activities to the GAD budget.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Immediately compute the correct tax liability for each affected employee and official. The corresponding under withheld amount should be reflected in a payroll adjustment and remitted to the BIR immediately. Any applicable penalties or interest should be to the account of the Municipal Accountant as the identified withholding agent in the BIR IRR.
Carefully examine DVs and ensure that taxes from taxable compensations of Municipal employees are duly withhold to avoid the same lapses in the future.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the HRM Officer develop guidelines, policies, standards, and procedures for the hiring, retention, and renewal of JO contracts, aligned with the Municipality’s Organizational Structure and Staffing Pattern ensuring non-duplication of functions and efficient use of government funds.

We commended the efforts of the Municipality to insure its motor vehicles against various risks such as accidents, theft, fire, and even acts of nature.
We also recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the Supply Officer to:
Prepare annually the consolidated Property Inventory Form listing all the insurable properties and other assets of the Municipality; and
Submit the consolidated PIF to the Audit Team Leader and the GIF, GSIS, not later than April 30 of each year as required in COA Circular No. 2018-002 dated May 31, 2018 as basis for the assessment of general insurance coverage over all insurable assets, properties and interests of the Municipality with the GIF of the GSIS.

We recommended to the management to:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to reverse the adjusting entry made in JEV# 100-2024-12-0397 as it was not supported by proper documentation and reconciliation. Submit the JEV drawn for the adjustment made to the audit team for verification.
Stop, henceforth, the incorrect accounting practice of making direct adjustments to the books of accounts based on the total balance presented in RPCPPE. Instead, adhere religiously and speedily to the proper reconciliation procedure enumerated in Section 6.3 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 on one-time cleansing to be undertaken collaboratively among the records of the Inventory Committee, Accounting, and GSO.
During reconciliation, address the issues on PPE accounts with unidentifiable “beginning balances”, temporary accounts, or without an appropriate/specific description in the subsidiary ledger accounts.
Furthermore, we reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendations to the management to:
Instruct the Inventory Committee, the GSO-Designate, and the Municipal Accountant to continue its conduct of physical count each year and to exert extra efforts in reconciling the reports per physical inventory, per GSO, and per accounting records.
The management to avail the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts in accordance with COA Circular 2020-006 to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition, and accountability.
Establish good practices on supply and property management by extending efforts to update and maintain complete records for supply and property accountabilities, such as property tags and property cards per category of PPE.

We recommended to the management to do the following:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the dredging cost improperly treated as asset, instead as direct expense, by drawing a journal entry voucher supported with necessary supporting document/s. Likewise, submit the same to the audit team for evaluation.
Direct the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to properly treat desilting/dredging project as a direct expense for similar future transaction, if any.

We recommended to the management to require the Municipal Accountant to:
Adhere faithfully to the accrual basis of accounting for insurance and fidelity bond premiums and to draw Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to effect the necessary adjusting entries supported with verifiable working papers, in order to enhance the fair presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements.
Ensure, henceforth, that all LTO registration payments are correctly debited to the Taxes, Duties, and Licenses account and duly supported with adequate documents.
Prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the overstatement of Accounts Payable in relation to Insurance Expense.

We recommended to the management to:
Direct the Municipal Accountant and her subordinates to immediately submit all outstanding Disbursement Vouchers and General Journal accounts (including all Journal Entry Vouchers), in compliance with applicable rules and regulations to avoid issuance of Notice of Suspension/Disallowance, if warranted, and modification of auditor’s opinion on financial statements due to audit scope limitation. Moreover, the Municipal Accountant to ensure that complete supporting documents are completely forwarded to COA for post-audit and verification.
Implement adequate measures that will enable the agency to meet such deadlines, such as but not limited to expediting the segregation and compilation of financial documents.

We recommended to the management to do the following:
Require the BAC to submit immediately to the audit team the said consultancy service contract, along with its pertinent supporting documents, to facilitate the auditorial, legal and technical review thereof. Henceforth, ensure the timely submission of similar contract documents within five (5) working days from its perfection, in accordance with COA Circular 2009-001.
Direct the Municipal Accountant and the BAC to submit immediately to this audit team the lacking necessary documentation in item nos. I and II as observed and discussed above, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-001 and Annex H, Section 7(b) of the IRR of RA 9184, to facilitate the continuance of our post-audit.

We recommended to the management the following:
Suspend the grant of union leave to employees pending a written clarification or legal opinion from the Civil Service Commission to determine its legality and validity. Once obtained, please provide a certified copy to the audit team.
Discontinue the grant of union leave if no basis affirming its legality and validity is established, and amend the CNA with the Employees’ association to remove the provision related to union leave for LGU employees.

We recommended to the management to do the following:
The Municipal Accounting Office to –
Ensure that all withheld taxes are intactly remitted to BIR on the following month, on or before the due date.
Initiate the timely preparation of DV for the transfer of remittance shares of LDF, SEF and TF in favor of GF Proper prior to the actual remittance to BIR. Ensure further that the MTO timely issued check for the purpose.
Conduct periodic reconciliation of any difference/s noted between monthly withholding of VAT and EWT against subsequent remittances.
Demand the immediate submission of DVs/payrolls and supporting documents for transactions paid out from the cash advances of the Cash Disbursing Officer at least before the end of the month, in order to resolve the differences noted between the amounts of withholding and remittance.
Strictly adhere with the suggested journal entries in recording BIR-related transactions.
The Municipal Treasurer’s Office to –
Timely prepare and issue checks for the transfer of remittance shares of LDF, SEF and TF in favor of GF Proper prior to the actual remittance to BIR by the latter.
Timely prepare and issue checks for tax remittances in favor of BIR on or before the due date.
Correct its improper practice of delaying the collection and deposit of checks issued by TF, SEF and LDF in favor of the GF Proper.
Stop its incorrect practice of issuing OR in favor of GF Proper for checks issued by LDF.

We recommended to the management to do the following:
Submit immediately the following lacking documents for our further review and audit action:
Approved DTR/Report or Duly Accomplished Time Record or employee or travel report
Copy of Special Order from the agency/Department Head covering the assignment to hazardous/difficult areas.
Monthly certification from the concerned Department Head showing the degrees of risk to hazards, the number of days of actual exposure and the corresponding amounts entitled to each personnel, duly noted by the Local Chief Executive.
Authority for entitlement to collect the benefit approved by Agency Head
Submit immediately valid justification to the audit team for the noted improper computations; otherwise, refund the overpayment of hazard pay and subsistence allowance on or before the end of the first quarter of 2025.
Adhere with the proper computation for hazard pay and subsistence allowance claims. Additionally, the concerned offices (MHO, MSWDO, HRMO and MACCO) should exercise caution in computing and reviewing the correct amount of benefits rightfully due to PSWs and PHWs to avoid undue payments.

We recommended to the management to do the following:
Submit immediately the lacking documents enumerated in Annex P of this audit report to comply with the documentary requirements and to facilitate the continuance of our post-audit for these disbursed transactions.
Review and orient the personnel in charge in preparing and reviewing such claims on the provision of EO 77, particularly those stipulating that the maximum DTE is determined by the regional location of the destination, and that the DTE for the date of departure is limited to 50% of the full component, in order to avoid over claim of travelling expenses.
Furthermore, we reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendations to the management to stop the practice of granting cash advances for travelling expenses in favor to various municipal officials and employees which are improperly cash advanced by one accountable officer, especially to those elective officials. This type of cash advance should be a personal accountability of each personnel.

We recommended to the management to:
Submit immediately to the audit team the noted lacking documents to substantiate the disbursed transactions. Henceforth, ensure that complete supporting documents are attached as prescribed under the Memorandum of Agreement, DOH AO No. 2022-001, and DOH-DBM JAO No. 2023-001.
For those claims with noted over/underpayments, review and recompute the correctness of the payroll amount of each recipient. Immediately submit valid justification/s and additional documents to the audit team for the finalization of our audit action. If warranted, initiate the recovery of overpaid amounts from recipients and promptly disburse those funds to the recipients who were underpaid to rectify the deficiencies in the disbursements. Any unutilized amount should be refunded to the source agency.

We recommended to the management to:
Submit the lacking documentary requirements and valid justifications for other noted observations for CY 2024 transactions, as mandated by COA Circular No. 2012-001.
Submit documents to the audit team proving those multiple mobile plans under one official and those under the name of former employees have been surrendered/turned over to the LGU. Such documents could be ICS or PAR and a certification issued by the current user of the mobile number indicating he/she is currently using and has custody over such mobile plan.
Develop and implement immediately the internal guidelines/policies governing the grant of communication expenses for officials and employees. Management is advised to read and adopt the guidelines reflected in DBM Circular No. 2024-2 dated August 2, 2024, particularly the allowed personnel, ceiling amount, guidelines on reimbursement based on actual work performance, and the documentary requirements (such as the self-certification). The said internal guidelines should be applied for transactions effective January 1, 2025.
We reiterated the prior year recommendation that the Municipal Mayor, together with the LDRRM Officer ensure optimal utilization of LDRRM Fund thru implementation of all approved PP

We recommended to the management to remind the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to prepare, henceforth, reliable SCBAA and SAAOB reports by including as part of obligations the already transferred unspent appropriations for LDRRMF- MOOE which have been treated already as a trust liability in the TF’s books of accounts at year-end. Further, require them to prepare a corrected SCBAA and SAAOB for CY 2023 for the management’s consumption/reference, supported with the necessary Obligation Requests for the said transfer, so as not to improperly treat such balances as surplus available for supplemental budget.

Likewise, the Municipal Accountant to draw adjusting journal entries, supported with working paper/s, to correct the overstatements incurred pertaining to the improper inclusion as trust fund the unspent capital outlay balances. Instead, this should be treated as continuing appropriations under the records of the Municipal Budget Officer, pursuant to Section 5.1.11 of COA Circular 2012-002
observations.reason:
JEV and its supporting documents for the recommended adjusting journal entry has not yet transmitted to the audit team by management despite its commitment during the exit conference on April 8, 2025.

Moreover, the audit team noticed that the unspent balances for CY 2024 to be transferred and recorded as Trust Liabilities -DRRMF were erroneously recorded under GF instead to TF.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the management to:
a. Require the Municipal Accountant to adhere with the proper timing and recording of the transactions involving RPT and SET as prescribed by Sections 20 and 36, Volume 1 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs;

b. Require the same personnel to prepare adjusting entry, supported with verifiable working paper/s, to record the amount of receivables and deferrals from delinquent taxpayers based on the Certified List of Delinquent Taxpayers (CLTD) derived from the Real Property Tax Account Register (RPTAR) prepared by the Municipal Treasurer as at December 31, 2023; and,

c. Instruct the Municipal Assessor, the Municipal Treasurer, and the Municipal Accountant to coordinate methods and measures of reconciliation to arrive at the correct real property tax and special education tax collectible balances, in order to enhance the reliability and accuracy of the financial statement accounts.

We recommended to the management to require the Municipal Accountant to adhere religiously with the correct procedure for computing depreciation expenses in accordance with Section 4(o), Chapter 1, Volume 1 of the NGAS Manual LGUs. Likewise, required the same to prepare an adjusting entry, supported with verifiable working papers, to accurately record the affected depreciation expenses and accumulated depreciation for all PPE additions in CY 2023 and submit the same to this audit team for our review and evaluation.

We recommended to the management to:

a. Instruct the Acting Municipal Assessor and the General Services Officer to coordinate in expediting the transfer of acquired real properties in favor to the name of the municipality by securing the TCTs pursuant to COA Circular 92-386 to avoid possible land disputes in the future.

b. Remind the designated General Services Officer to take custody of TCTs and other related documents.

c. Require the Municipal Accountant to undertake an in-depth analysis and reconciliation of the Land account to enhance the completeness and accuracy of the said affected account and to provide a separate relevant disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements as required by IPSAS 17 that would be beneficial in the decision-making of the users of the financial statements.
observations.reason: The identified parcels of land are still in the process of application for titling. No TCTs under the custody of the GSO while no separate note disclosure in the CY 2024 Notes to Financial Statements as required by IPSAS 17.

Compliance with the audit recommendation is still on-going. The disposal procedure has not yet been completed, specifically the appraisal and authority from SB.

During the exit conference, the GSO alleged that various documents were already prepared, such as IIRUP, Waste Materials Report, list of missing parts, stencils, and colored photographs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to the management to strictly implement a policy mandating personnel involved to prioritize and expedite the processing of payment of electricity and other utility bills. Moreover, required individuals found directly responsible for such delays to personally bear the cost of surcharge and not against government funds.

We recommended to the management to:
Stop the practice of engaging consultancy services thru appointment contracts. Instead, adhere with the provisions of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and Section 6.2.2 of COA-DBM JC No. 2, s. 2020 to give the public the best possible advantage through open competition.

Review the need for consultants in recurring government projects where extensive technical expertise might not be required and/or enhance the skills of regular employees to enable them to manage these projects independently, if warranted.

Evaluate whether the LGU will continually need the skills offered by the consultants and contemplate the possibility of hiring them as employees for purposes of economy and efficiency, if warranted.

We recommended to the management to stop the improper practice of granting honoraria to individuals performing regular full-time functions under the procurement unit and to the invited NGO observers. Instead, the management may consider granting overtime pay or per diem to the former and latter, respectively, if warranted.

Likewise, we recommended to the Municipal Accountant to submit immediately to the audit team the noted lacking documents to substantiate the disbursed transactions. Henceforth, ensured that complete supporting documents are attached as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2023-004.

We recommended to the management to immediately submit to the audit team the lacking documents for our further review and evaluation, most importantly all those motor vehicles not issued with Property Acknowledgement Receipts (PAR). Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all payments for motor vehicles are duly supported with complete documentary requirements.

We recommended to the management to stop the practice of splitting procurements serving the same purpose and same timing, thereby bypassing the proper procurement process. Instead, faithfully adhere with the provisions of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to give the public the best possible advantage through open competition.

We recommended to the management to meticulously and judiciously plan the procurement process of the agency by consolidating the quarterly requirements for similar procurement items as prescribed under Section 7 of the revised IRR of RA 9184. Specifically, required the end-users to early prepare their respective PPMPs for the year and submit to the BAC, thru the BAC Secretariat, for consolidation purposes. Further, strictly undertake agency procurement in accordance with the approved APP, including the approved changes thereto, as explicitly stated in Section 7.2 of the same IRR.

We recommended to the management to strictly require the BAC Secretariat to furnish all the perfected contracts, along with their related documents, in earliest time possible and to meticulously review the completeness and orderliness of the awarded contracts based on the provided contract review and technical review checklists prior submission to the audit team. Further, submit immediately the enumerated documents and/or valid justifications for some noted observations for our auditorial and legal review. 

More so, submit immediately the complete documentary requirements for the technical review of those infrastructure-related contracts using the checklist provided by the audit team, in which such documents are relevant to facilitate the technical evaluation thereof to be conducted by the COA Regional Technical Audit Group (RTAG).

Finally, the management to extend utmost priority and attention to address the concerns of the BAC Office vested with crucial functions for a more efficient and effective delivery of government service.
observations.reason: The submission of lacking documents was overlooked by management due to the transfer of the former BAC Secretariat to other government agency in CY 2024. However, the management expressed its compliance commitment during the exit conference on April 8, 2025.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the management to require the HRM Officer to strictly impose that, prior the rendition of actual service, a job order worker should effect his/her full signature on the job order contract. The same document should be used as reference for their review of the authenticity of signatures effected in the monthly Daily Time Record and accomplishment report prepared by a job order worker for payroll preparation and wage payment purposes.

Moreover, the management to instruct the Municipal Accountant to require the perfected contracts as an important documentary requirement for wage payments.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation to the management to instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to revisit COA Circular 2012-002 and review its pertinent provisions particularly on the Illustrative Accounting Entries for LDRRMF, the proper timing of recording the “Trust Liability-DRRMF” account in the Trust Fund books, and even the budgetary rules and regulations relating to DRRMF appropriations.

Further, we recommended the Municipal Accountant to conduct detailed analysis to establish the correct amount of “Trust Liabilities-LDRRMF” account, including the setting up of the required subsidiary ledger accounts. Afterwards, she should draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to effect the necessary adjustments to be made, supported with all the relevant working papers and supporting documents, and submit a copy of the same to the Office of the Auditor for our evaluation and post-audit in compliance to this audit observation.

We recommended to the management to ensure that disbursement vouchers for infrastructure-related disbursements in favor to contractors should be sufficiently and adequately attached with the required documents as prescribed under Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001. Specifically, proper coordination between the Municipal Engineering Office and the Municipal Accounting Office should be made in the processing of these claims. The latter office should meticulously review and ensure that these pertinent documents are completely attached to DVs prior disbursement.

Moreover, we recommended management to submit immediately to the audit team the lacking supporting documents and valid justification/s on other noted observations for our further review and evaluation.
observations.reason: The submission of lacking documents was not prioritized by management but expressed its compliance commitment during the exit conference on April 8, 2025.

Management still granted cash advances to one employee improperly accommodating multiple personalities, while the Municipal Accountant is still tracing and reconciling balances in the “Advances to Officers and Employees” ledger with negative and unidentified balances of subsidiary ledger accounts. This finding has also been reiterated in the current year’s audit observation as presented in Part II of this audit report for CY 2024.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the management to do the following:

Submit the lacking supporting documents particularly the DTRs, and accomplishment reports;

Explain the variance in the number of days per DTRs vs per Summary List; and

Ensure that advances for special purpose/time-bound undertakings are recorded under the Advances to Special Disbursing Officer Account.

We recommended the following:

 1.  Issue demand letters to all accountable officers whether they are still connected or separated from the agency. In cases where the individual is already separated from the agency, demand letters should be sent to their current affiliated agencies or their last known residence via registered mail with return card.

 2.  For receivables which remained outstanding for 10 years or more, filing for requests for write off supported by necessary documentation (see COA Circular 2016-005) for submission to COA should be initiated.

 3.  In case of the outstanding fund transfers which remain unliquidated beyond the agreed time of liquidation, review of the Memorandum of Agreement should be done and the Municipality should send follow up letters to implementing agencies and demand immediate liquidation or refund thereof. Succeeding grants for fund transfers should be suspended until prior liquidation of the outstanding receivables consistent with existing rules and regulations.

 4.  Finally, if sufficient evidence as to the impairment of the receivables is obtained after exhausting all remedies, provision of impairment loss should be facilitated.
observations.reason: Management failed to submit proof of issuance of demand letters and request for write off for dormant receivable accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended to require the Municipal Accountant to carefully evaluate the original entries of the two reciprocal accounts and remove those that cannot be validated through appropriate accounting entries
observations.reason: On-going reconciliation by the accounting office. There is still noted material variance between said reciprocal accounts.

a) Notify the payees concerned at least one month before a check becomes
stale;
b) Upon presentation by the payees of the stale checks, physically cancel and
replace checks in accordance with Section 59 (3) of MNGAS for LGUs; and
c) Prepare at yearend a complete Schedule of Unreleased Checks together
with the Report of Checks Issued and submit to the Municipal Accountant
as her basis in drawing a journal entry voucher restoring the cash
equivalent to the unreleased checks and recognizing the appropriate
payable/ liability accounts.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a) Cancel all stale checks and revert to the Cash in Bank Account and record
the Accounts Payable account as prescribed in Section 61 of MNGAS for
LGUs; and
b) Revert all unreleased checks to the Cash in Bank account and recognize
appropriate liability accounts at the end of every year and reverse the
entries made in the ensuing year.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer:
a. Conduct verification of prior years’ PPAs which are recorded in the CIP
account; and
b. Collate the pertinent documents relative to the CIP accounts and provide
Project Status Report as well as Certificate of Project Completion and
Inspection and Acceptance Report to the Municipal Accountant as her
basis in the transfer of completed PPAs under the CIP account to its
appropriate asset accounts;

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Transfer completed projects to appropriate PPE account; and
b. Make the necessary adjustments relative to the current and prior years'
unrecorded depreciation expenses and impairment losses on the completed
projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Assessor expedite the processing of
Certificate of Title of the untitled lots in order to establish absolute ownership
and protect the legal interest of the Municipality.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure and establish lot
ownership, in the name of the Municipality, before construction of any
structures and land improvements.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace and reconcile
the amounts recorded in the books with the source documents and effect
adjustments, if necessary.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor together with the Municipal
Engineer, MPDC and other concerned personnel ensure that the PPAs to be
funded out of the 20% DF are well-planned, as well as procurement-and-
implementation-ready such that the factors that may impede implementation,
such as weather condition and accessibility of the site, shall be considered.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, MPDC and Municipal
Engineer fast-track and monitor the implementation of the PPAs funded out
of the 20% Development Fund both for current and prior years’ balances to
achieve the purpose of the fund.

It is also recommended that appropriations of all PPAs which are no longer
feasible and have not yet been implemented, be reverted back to the
unappropriated surplus of the 20% DF for re-appropriation to other
development projects pursuant to budgetary rules.

We reiterated the prior year recommendation that the Municipal Mayor,
together with the LDRRM Officer ensure optimal utilization of LDRRM Fund
thru implementation of all approved PPAs incorporated in the LDRRMF
Investment Plan.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor together with the LDRRM
Council re-appropriate or re-align appropriation of PPAs which are no longer
feasible to other LDRRM priority projects.

Moreover, we recommended that the LDRRM Officer send letters to the
source agencies of financial assistance received and inquire whether the grants
be utilized for other purpose since the intended objective of the funds is
already difficult to attain due to the passing of time.

Lastly, we recommended that the LDRRM Council review and update its CY
2024 LDRRMF Investment Plan and include new expenditure items to be
funded out from the unexpended LDRRM Fund balances of the previous years
which were transferred to the Special Trust Fund account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, thru the OIC- Municipal
Agriculturist, send letter to DOST Region 8 to inquire whether the funds
downloaded for the DOST- CEST Program can still be utilized for the purpose
considering that the duration for implementation provided in the MOA has
ended. Otherwise, return the funds to the source agency.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare disbursement
voucher for the return of the unexpended amount of ₱412.27 to the DA.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant transfer and
reclassify the amounts of ₱523.00 and ₱245.28 to the Subsidy from National
Government account – General Fund of the Municipality if return of these
funds is no longer required by the Source Agency.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor thru the LDRRM Officer comply with
the mandate of the NTC to secure Permit to Purchase and the dealer’s Permit to Sell
in support of succeeding purchase of radio communications equipment.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant secure and check the
completeness and validity of the permits required for the same transactions/
expenditures in the future.

We recommended that the Municipal Health Officer (MHO) and the
claimants:
i) secure Certification by the Secretary of the DOH that the place of
assignment/travel is a strife-torn/embattled/disease-infested/distress or
isolated areas/stations, or areas declared under state of calamity or
emergency, or with volcano activity or eruption, and
ii) attach the complete supporting documents to the disbursement
vouchers.

We likewise recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the
Certification by the Secretary of the DOH as prescribed in COA Circular No.
2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 and strictly observe the guidelines on the grant of
hazard pay, subsistence and laundry allowances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, Municipal Accountant and Municipal
Treasurer:
a. Stop the payment of confidential expenses thru reimbursement. The release and
utilization of the funds shall be thru a cash advance by a Special Disbursing
Officer or the Head of the Agency, and
b. Comply with the aforementioned reporting requirements, upon request and
grant of cash advance and its liquidation.

We also recommended that the Local Peace and Order Council with the
Municipal Mayor as the chairman to convene such that release and utilization
of the Confidential Funds shall be covered by a resolution duly approved by
2/3 votes of its total membership.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
a) Require all the Collectors to prepare the Report of Collections and
Remittances daily and remit their collections to the Liquidating Officer at
the close of each business day or in the case of collectors assigned to the
field, where travel time from their places of assignment to the Municipal
Treasurer’s Office is more than one day, turnover of collections shall be
made at least once a week or as soon as the collections reach P5,000.00; and
b) Strictly comply with the regulations prescribed in MNGAS for LGUs to
safeguard the funds of the Municipality.

We recommended to the management to remind the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to prepare, henceforth, reliable SCBAA and SAAOB reports by including as part of obligations the already transferred unspent appropriations for LDRRMF- MOOE which have been treated already as a trust liability in the TF’s books of accounts at year -end. Further, required them to prepare a corrected SCBAA and SAAOB for CY 2023 for the management’s consumption/reference, supported with the necessary Obligation Requests for the said transfer, so as not to improperly treat such balances as surplus available for supplemental budget.

Likewise, the Municipal Accountant to draw adjusting journal entries, supported with working paper/s, to correct the overstatements incurred pertaining to the improper inclusion as trust fund the unspent capital outlay balances. Instead, this should be treated as continuing appropriations under the records of the Municipal Budget Officer, pursuant to Section 5.1.11 of COA Circular 2012-002.

We recommended to the management to:
Require the Municipal Accountant to adhere with the proper timing and recording of the transactions involving RPT and SET as prescribed by Sections 20 and 36, Volume 1 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs;
Require the same personnel to prepare adjusting entry, supported with verifiable working paper/s, to record the amount of receivables and deferrals from delinquent taxpayers based on the Certified List of Delinquent Taxpayers (CLTD) derived from the Real Property Tax Account Register (RPTAR) prepared by the Municipal Treasurer as at December 31, 2023; and,
Instruct the Municipal Assessor, the Municipal Treasurer, and the Municipal Accountant to coordinate methods and measures of reconciliation to arrive at the correct real property tax and special education tax collectible balances, in order to enhance the reliability and accuracy of the financial statement accounts.

We recommended to the management to require the Municipal Accountant to adhere religiously with the correct procedure for computing depreciation expenses in accordance with Section 4(o), Chapter 1, Volume 1 of the NGAS Manual LGUs. Likewise, required the same to prepare an adjusting entry, supported with verifiable working papers, to accurately record the affected depreciation expenses and accumulated depreciation for all PPE additions in CY 2023 and submit the same to this audit team for our review and evaluation.

We recommended to the management to:
Instruct the Acting Municipal Assessor and the General Services Officer to coordinate in expediting the transfer of acquired real properties in favor to the name of the municipality by securing the TCTs pursuant to COA Circular 92-386 to avoid possible land disputes in the future.
Remind the designated General Services Officer to take custody of TCTs and other related documents.
Require the Municipal Accountant to undertake an in-depth analysis and reconciliation of the Land account to enhance the completeness and accuracy of the said affected account and to provide a separate relevant disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements as required by IPSAS 17 that would be beneficial in the decision-making of the users of the financial statements.

We recommended to the management to direct the Inventory Committee, the GSO-Designate, and the Municipal Accountant to continue its conduct of physical count each year and to exert extra efforts in reconciling the reports per physical inventory, per GSO, and per accounting records. Likewise, the management to avail the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts in accordance with COA Circular 2020-006 to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

Further, we recommended to the management to establish good practices on supply and property management by extending efforts to update and maintain complete records for supply and property accountabilities, such as Property Acknowledgment Receipt (PAR), Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS), and property cards per category of PPE.

Moreover, instructed the Municipal Accountant to draw Journal Entry Vouchers to drop in the books of accounts those items totaling ₱231,152.00 which did not qualify the PPE capitalization threshold of ₱50,000 and ensured, henceforth, that all property and equipment recorded in the books qualify as PPE pursuant to Item 4.2 of the same COA circular. Correspondingly, cancel the related PAR and issue instead ICS for the said items with cost below the said threshold.

We recommended to the management to strictly implement a policy mandating personnel involved to prioritize and expedite the processing of payment of electricity and other utility bills. Moreover, required individuals found directly responsible for such delays to personally bear the cost of surcharge and not against government funds.

We recommended to the management to:
Stop the practice of engaging consultancy services thru appointment contracts. Instead, adhere with the provisions of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and Section 6.2.2 of COA-DBM JC No. 2, s. 2020 to give the public the best possible advantage through open competition.
Review the need for consultants in recurring government projects where extensive technical expertise might not be required and/or enhance the skills of regular employees to enable them to manage these projects independently, if warranted.
Evaluate whether the LGU will continually need the skills offered by the consultants and contemplate the possibility of hiring them as employees for purposes of economy and efficiency, if warranted.

We recommended to the management to stop the improper practice of granting honoraria to individuals performing regular full-time functions under the procurement unit and to the invited NGO observers. Instead, the management may consider granting overtime pay or per diem to the former and latter, respectively, if warranted.

Likewise, we recommended to the Municipal Accountant to submit immediately to the audit team the noted lacking documents to substantiate the disbursed transactions. Henceforth, ensured that complete supporting documents are attached as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2023-004.

We recommended to the management to immediately submit to the audit team the lacking documents for our further review and evaluation, most importantly all those motor vehicles not issued with Property Acknowledgement Receipts (PAR). Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all payments for motor vehicles are duly supported with complete documentary requirements.

We recommended to the management to stop the practice of splitting procurements serving the same purpose and same timing, thereby bypassing the proper procurement process. Instead, faithfully adhere with the provisions of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to give the public the best possible advantage through open competition.

We recommended to the management to meticulously and judiciously plan the procurement process of the agency by consolidating the quarterly requirements for similar procurement items as prescribed under Section 7 of the revised IRR of RA 9184. Specifically, required the end-users to early prepare their respective PPMPs for the year and submit to the BAC, thru the BAC Secretariat, for consolidation purposes. Further, strictly undertake agency procurement in accordance with the approved APP, including the approved changes thereto, as explicitly stated in Section 7.2 of the same IRR.

We recommended to the management to strictly require the BAC Secretariat to furnish all the perfected contracts, along with their related documents, in earliest time possible and to meticulously review the completeness and orderliness of the awarded contracts based on the provided contract review and technical review checklists prior submission to the audit team. Further, submit immediately the enumerated documents and/or valid justifications for some noted observations for our auditorial and legal review.

More so, submit immediately the complete documentary requirements for the technical review of those infrastructure-related contracts using the checklist provided by the audit team, in which such documents are relevant to facilitate the technical evaluation thereof to be conducted by the COA Regional Technical Audit Group (RTAG).

Finally, the management to extend utmost priority and attention to address the concerns of the BAC Office vested with crucial functions for a more efficient and effective delivery of government service.

We recommended to the management to require the HRM Officer to strictly impose that, prior the rendition of actual service, a job order worker should effect his/her full signature on the job order contract. The same document should be used as reference for their review of the authenticity of signatures effected in the monthly Daily Time Record and accomplishment report prepared by a job order worker for payroll preparation and wage payment purposes.

Moreover, the management to instruct the Municipal Accountant to require the perfected contracts as an important documentary requirement for wage payments.

We recommended to the management to require the Municipal Accountant to immediately draw Journal Entry Voucher (JEV), supported with verifiable documents and working papers, in order to record/adjust those valid reconciling items reflected in the BRS and submit to the audit team for review and evaluation. Moreover, ensured that reconciling items are recorded/adjusted in the books of accounts immediately upon detection. Further, required the Municipal Accountant, in close coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, to prepare BRS for those already dormant accounts. 

Furthermore, we recommended to the management to consider the closure of unnecessary and already dormant bank accounts, specifically TF-Regular, TF-INREMP Equity and TF-INREMP LP accounts maintained at LBP Velez and Puerto branches.

We recommended to the management to require the Municipal Treasurer to stop the incorrect practice of issuing official receipts for fund transfers made from GF-LDF to GF-Proper accounts. Instead, checks drawn in favor to the former account should be directly deposited and the corresponding validated deposit slip be attached to the corresponding disbursement voucher. 

Further, we required the Municipal Accountant to effect adjustments prospectively in the books of accounts by drawing Journal Entry Voucher (JEV), supported with working paper, in order to correct the affected financial accounts. Afterwards, submit to the audit team for our review and evaluation. Moreover, the management to prospectively ensure that proper accounting entries are made in recording those shares accruing to 20% LDF account.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation to the management to instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to revisit COA Circular 2012-002 and review its pertinent provisions particularly on the Illustrative Accounting Entries for LDRRMF, the proper timing of recording the “Trust Liability-DRRMF” account in the Trust Fund books, and even the budgetary rules and regulations relating to DRRMF appropriations. 

	Further, we recommended the Municipal Accountant to conduct detailed analysis to establish the correct amount of “Trust Liabilities-LDRRMF” account, including the setting up of the required subsidiary ledger accounts. Afterwards, she should draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to effect the necessary adjustments to be made, supported with all the relevant working papers and supporting documents, and submit a copy of the same to the Office of the Auditor for our evaluation and post-audit in compliance to this audit observation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the management to be prudent and judicious in exercising its fiscal responsibility and accountability over the disbursed funds, by strictly adhering to the proper obligation and disbursement processes. Specifically, the management should ensure that all DVs, payrolls and obligation requests (ObRs) are completely signed by proper authorities in order to delineate full accountability over the disbursed funds. If in case that a representative is authorized to sign on behalf of the proper official, he/she should print his name and full signature to the pertinent document and becomes fully accountable therefor. 

For those disbursements with unsigned DVs, payrolls and ObRs, submit a certification that the concerned authorized official failed to sign the document and is fully responsible and accountable thereof. The details of the disbursed transaction and the nature of his participation thereof should also be specified. Non-submission thereof may warrant issuance of audit suspensions or disallowance, as the case may be.

For those documents signed by an unknown representative, the management should submit a certified list of signature specimens of those authorized/allowed to sign in behalf of the concerned officials. Submit also a joint certification from the proper official and his/her representative that they are fully responsible and accountable to such disbursement. The details of the disbursed transaction and the nature of their participation should also be specified. Non-submission thereof may warrant issuance of audit suspensions or disallowance, as the case may be.

Further, implement a good internal control system to prevent incurrence of such improper disbursement practice with inadequate signatures.

Furthermore, the management to use the correct DV form as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2006-002 dated January 31, 2006

We recommended to the management to ensure that disbursement vouchers for infrastructure-related disbursements in favor to contractors should be sufficiently and adequately attached with the required documents as prescribed under Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001. Specifically, proper coordination between the Municipal Engineering Office and the Municipal Accounting Office should be made in the processing of these claims. The latter office should meticulously review and ensure that these pertinent documents are completely attached to DVs prior disbursement. 

Moreover, we recommended management to submit immediately to the audit team the lacking supporting documents and valid justification/s on other noted observations for our further review and evaluation.

Non-compliance thereof may warrant the issuance of audit suspension or disallowance, whichever may deem appropriate

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:

1.  Issue demand letters to all accountable officers whether they are still connected or separated from the agency. In cases where the individual is already separated from the agency, demand letters should be sent to their current affiliated agencies or their last known residence via registered mail with return card.

2.  For receivables which remained outstanding for 10 years or more, filing for requests for write off supported by necessary documentation (see COA Circular 2016-005) for submission to COA should be initiated.

3.  In case of the outstanding fund transfers which remain unliquidated beyond the agreed time of liquidation, review of the Memorandum of Agreement should be done and the Municipality should send follow up letters to implementing agencies and demand immediate liquidation or refund thereof. Succeeding grants for fund transfers should be suspended until prior liquidation of the outstanding receivables consistent with existing rules and regulations.

4.  Finally, if sufficient evidence as to the impairment of the receivables is obtained after exhausting all remedies, provision of impairment loss should be facilitated.

observations.recommendation:
1.12 We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to require:
1. The Inventory Committee to conduct an actual physical count of all the
PPE to determine the actual existence and proper valuation of the same;
2. The Municipal Accountant to recheck the recognized PPE items without the residual
value and adjust accordingly the depreciation computation as prescribed in IPSAS 17
for PPE; and
3. Ensure that the physical inventory be undertaken annually and submit the RPCPPE
to the COA Auditor concerned on or before January 31 of each.

2.13 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
1. The Municipal Accountant to recognize Accounts Payable that are
consummated transactions with complete documentation to prove that
these are valid liabilities;
2. The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to promptly submit the complete
bidding documents to the Municipal Account to facilitate the prompt
payments of obligations incurred by the Municipality; and
3. The Municipal Accountant to initiate actions for the proper disposition of
the long outstanding payables.

3.20 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to instruct the OIC - Municipal Treasurer
to:
a) prepare the annual Schedule of the Delinquent Stallholders and Lessees as at year-
end and furnish the same to the Municipal Accountant;
b) prepare Demand Letters indicating the total unpaid monthly rentals and the penalties
thereof to all delinquent stallholders; and
c) prepare a waiver of rental fees for the refund of repair expenses incurred for the
repair of their designated market stalls.

3.21 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant record in the books of accounts
the receivables on the rentals from the Market Stall and the Government Lots, based on
the report forwarded by the Municipal Treasurer and the corresponding income earned
therefrom.

4.10 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Planning and Development
Coordinator (MPDC) to plan well the implementation of the PPAs such
that the factors that may impede construction, such as weather and
accessibility of the site, shall be considered.
b. Ensure that the development projects to be funded out of the 20% DF are
well-planned, as well as procurement-and-implementation-ready to
achieve the objectives of the fund.
c. Direct the MPDC to fast track and monitor the implementation of the
PPAs funded out of the 20% Development Fund both Current and Prior
Years continuing balances.

5.18 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. LDRRMO Head to ensure optimal utilization of funds thru
implementation of all approved PPAs incorporated in the LDRRM Plan
and integrated in the approved AIP;
b. LDRRM Council to review and update the LDRRMFIP annually to
include activities to be funded from the unexpended QRF and DRRMF –
MOOE of the previous year which were transferred to the Special Trust
Fund; and
c. Municipal Accountant to process payment for the utilization of the
LDRRMF only for programs, projects and activities which are included in
the LDRRMFP and AIP or those included in the approved Supplemental
AIP to ensure legality of the transactions.

6.13 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to effectively enforce the liquidation of fund transfers once PPAs
are completed including the remittance of the unspent balance to National
Treasury.

7.11 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a.) Create clear-cut policies and guidelines to address the issue on the necessity
and propriety of hiring Casual/JOs taking into consideration the existing regular
employees;
b.) Limit the hiring of personnel under Casual/Job Order contracts to those
extremely necessary and deploy only to offices that actually are in need of their
services;
c.) Require Job Order employees to submit primary personal identification,
undergo interview and the HRMO to evaluate the performance of each JO to
mitigate the risk of hiring unqualified personnel; and
d.) Incorporate in the contract the number of man hours, quantity, and duration
of the work to be performed by the JO personnel and require them to submit
quantified accomplishments reports to support payments of wages.

8.14 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Nurse/designated personnel by the Rural Health Physician (RHP) to:
1. prepare and maintain an accurate report on the Summary/List of vaccines
received and distributed;
2. ensure proper monitoring on the movement of COVID-19 vaccines thru
reconciliation with Provincial Health Services Office (PHSO) as to the correct
number of vaccines received by the RHU based on complete supporting
documents; and
3. coordinate with the PHSO as to the scheduling of the retrieval of expired
vaccines from the RHU.
b. RHP to properly plan the utilization and distribution of COVID-19 vaccines,
including nearly expiring vaccines, to minimize or prevent wastages thereof.

9.10 We recommended to the Municipal Mayor, through the BAC to submit
written justification why award of contract was made to two bidders despite of
delays in its on-going contracts as disclosed in the Statement of All On-going
Government and Private Contracts Including Contracts Awarded but Not Yet
Started.

9.11 We also recommended to the BAC to submit written justification why a
recommendation for an award of contract was made despite the
misrepresentation of data by non-disclosure in its eligibility documents all
previously awarded contracts.

9.12 Lastly, in future procurements of infrastructure projects, we recommended to
the BAC to thoroughly scrutinize the post-qualification of the bidding
documents and to require, where necessary, documents to comprehensively
undertake the post-qualification of bidders.

We recommended to the management to require the Municipal Accountant to immediately draw Journal Entry Voucher (JEV), supported with verifiable documents and working papers, in order to record/adjust those valid reconciling items reflected in the BRS and submit to the audit team for review and evaluation. Moreover, ensured that reconciling items are recorded/adjusted in the books of accounts immediately upon detection. Further, required the Municipal Accountant, in close coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, to prepare BRS for those already dormant accounts.
Furthermore, we recommended to the management to consider the closure of unnecessary and already dormant bank accounts, specifically TF-Regular, TF-INREMP Equity and TF-INREMP LP accounts maintained at LBP Velez and Puerto branches.

We recommended to the management to require the Municipal Treasurer to stop the incorrect practice of issuing official receipts for fund transfers made from GF-LDF to GF-Proper accounts. Instead, checks drawn in favor to the former account should be directly deposited and the corresponding validated deposit slip be attached to the corresponding disbursement voucher.
Further, we required the Municipal Accountant to effect adjustments prospectively in the books of accounts by drawing Journal Entry Voucher (JEV), supported with working paper, in order to correct the affected financial accounts. Afterwards, submit to the audit team for our review and evaluation. Moreover, the management to prospectively ensure that proper accounting entries are made in recording those shares accruing to 20% LDF account.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation to the management to instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to revisit COA Circular 2012-002 and review its pertinent provisions particularly on the Illustrative Accounting Entries for LDRRMF, the proper timing of recording the “Trust Liability-DRRMF” account in the Trust Fund books, and even the budgetary rules and regulations relating to DRRMF appropriations.
Further, we recommended the Municipal Accountant to conduct detailed analysis to establish the correct amount of “Trust Liabilities-LDRRMF” account, including the setting up of the required subsidiary ledger accounts. Afterwards, she should draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to effect the necessary adjustments to be made, supported with all the relevant working papers and supporting documents, and submit a copy of the same to the Office of the Auditor for our evaluation and post-audit in compliance to this audit observation.

We recommended to the management, through the Municipal Accountant, to immediately prepare and submit to the audit team the required CY 2022 financial reports, supported with schedules, for 20% LDF as a special account under the general fund.
Moreover, we recommended to the management to push through the merging of LDF bank accounts into one account only while effect the immediate closure of the other accounts, for easy monitoring and reconciliation of the transactions under 20% LDF.

We recommended to the Local Chief Executive and the local sanggunian to effect immediately the proper closure of all three (3) PRDP bank accounts maintained by LGU-Malitbog at LBP-Puerto Branch since these are considered “unnecessary” bank accounts already. Further, the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to coordinate closely to proceed with the reconciliation of two (2) bank accounts not prepared with BRS since October 2022.

We recommended to the management to be prudent and judicious in exercising its fiscal responsibility and accountability over the disbursed funds, by strictly adhering to the proper obligation and disbursement processes. Specifically, the management should ensure that all DVs, payrolls and obligation requests (ObRs) are completely signed by proper authorities in order to delineate full accountability over the disbursed funds. If in case that a representative is authorized to sign on behalf of the proper official, he/she should print his name and full signature to the pertinent document and becomes fully accountable therefor.
For those disbursements with unsigned DVs, payrolls and ObRs, submit a certification that the concerned authorized official failed to sign the document and is fully responsible and accountable thereof. The details of the disbursed transaction and the nature of his participation thereof should also be specified. Non-submission thereof may warrant issuance of audit suspensions or disallowance, as the case may be.
For those documents signed by an unknown representative, the management should submit a certified list of signature specimens of those authorized/allowed to sign in behalf of the concerned officials. Submit also a joint certification from the proper official and his/her representative that they are fully responsible and accountable to such disbursement. The details of the disbursed transaction and the nature of their participation should also be specified. Non-submission thereof may warrant issuance of audit suspensions or disallowance, as the case may be.
Further, implement a good internal control system to prevent incurrence of such improper disbursement practice with inadequate signatures.
Furthermore, the management to use the correct DV form as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2006-002 dated January 31, 2006

We recommended to the management to ensure that disbursement vouchers for infrastructure-related disbursements in favor to contractors should be sufficiently and adequately attached with the required documents as prescribed under Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001. Specifically, proper coordination between the Municipal Engineering Office and the Municipal Accounting Office should be made in the processing of these claims. The latter office should meticulously review and ensure that these pertinent documents are completely attached to DVs prior disbursement.
Moreover, we recommended management to submit immediately to the audit team the lacking supporting documents and valid justification/s on other noted observations for our further review and evaluation.

We recommended to the management to require the BAC, thru its secretariat, to immediately furnish the audit team all the perfected contracts, along with its related documents, as soon as possible, to avoid delay in the legal and auditorial review and the technical aspect of said contracts. Non-compliance thereof may warrant the issuance of audit suspension or disallowance, whichever may deem appropriate.

We recommended to the management to require the Municipal Budget Officer to correct its improper obligation process by obligating the total contract price against specific appropriation of projects to be implemented thru straight contract. Moreover, the Bids and Awards Committee to require the preparation of Obligation Request before the award of contract as prescribed by RA 9184.

We recommended to the management to charge AIFCS payments against a special cash advance exclusively made for such purpose, and not against petty cash fund. Observe the proper granting, liquidating, and recording of these transactions pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended to the management to require the Municipal Treasurer to strictly comply with the requirements under COA Circular No. 92-389 to maintain a sound internal control in the disbursement of government funds thru the perforation of all supporting documents of paid Disbursement Vouchers and payrolls to avoid their reuse.

We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to require:

In cases where the individual is already separated from the agency, demand letters should be sent to their current affiliated agencies or their last known residence via registered mail with return card.

2.  For receivables which remained outstanding for 10 years or more, filing for requests for write off supported by necessary documentation (see COA Circular 2016-005) for submission to COA should be initiated.

4.  Finally, if sufficient evidence as to the impairment of the receivables is obtained after exhausting all remedies, provision of impairment loss should be facilitated.

We recommended the following:

In cases where the individual is already separated from the agency, demand letters should be sent to their current affiliated agencies or their last known residence via registered mail with return card.

Documents for CY 2019 and 2020 showing the specific ‘cost-cutting and systems improvement measures of management with detailed computation of the resulting available allotments broken down as to specific MOOE budgetary line items;
Documents showing the LGU’s accomplishment of the minimum threshold of 70% of all targeted programs/activities/projects as of September 30, 2019 and September 30, 2020;

Other necessary documents (Detailed report of the MOOE sources of funds for CNA bonuses for CY 2019 and 2020 which sourced solely from the available balances of allowable MOOE under existing guidelines).
Strictly grant CNA Incentives following the existing guidelines; and
Empower implementation of projects under the 20% Local Development Fund.

Recommendation:

Recommendation:

Nonetheless, the Municipal Accountant should carefully examine the supporting documents on all payments relative to the procurement of goods, infrastructure and services to determine penalties once delay on delivery or project completion.

Recommendation:
We recommended to the management to establish a sound internal control in the purchase and usage of fuel to avoid misuse of the asset. Also, submit all charge invoices, billing statement and actual consumption report to support payments to 68,000 liters of fuel withdrawn. Thereafter, strictly observe that all payments to fuel are properly supported with valid documents.

Recommendation:
We recommended to the management that all cash advances for labor payments should be supported with approved payrolls and other documents enumerated in COA Circular 2012-001 in order to validate the accuracy and propriety of the amount and minimize excess cash advances.

Recommendation:
We recommended to the management to establish a guideline on the grant of AICS to indigent using the DWSD Memorandum Circular as reference.

Recommendation:

Recommendation:
We recommended to the management to submit justifications to explain the following:
Goods not in the Purchase Order were paid by management even though these goods were much more expensive than the goods agreed upon in the purchase order which resulted to lesser relief goods for distribution to target beneficiaries.
The LGU did not penalized the failure of the supplier, Neotrinity Corporation, to deliver the goods as agreed in the purchase order.
The Inspection Officer/Property Custodian certified that she inspected and received various goods in the Purchase Order totaling ₱1,218,253.95 which were not actually delivered by the supplier. Also, she did not declare the actual goods that she received totaling ₱1,218,253.50.
Also, we recommended to the management to establish a clear process flow for all disbursements and to ensure that succeeding inspections and acceptances of goods are grounded on objectivity and the truth.

Recommendation:

observations.recommendation:
a. Require the officials concerned to strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 111 of PD 1445, in order to adequately furnish the accurate information of the PPE of the LGU.
b. Require the Municipal Accountant-designate and the General Supply Officer/Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the difference of P22,489,138.85 between the balances per books with the physical count as of December 31, 2014 in order to reflect an accurate balance of the PPE.
c. Require both the Accounting Office and Property Officer to reclassify tangible assets if found during the reconciliation with serviceable life of more than one year but small enough to be considered as PPE, thus, small tangible items with estimated useful life of more than one year shall be recorded as inventories upon acquisition and as expense upon issuance, per Items 2.2 of COA Circular 2005-002 dated April 14, 2005.

a) All procurement should be meticulously and judiciously planned by the procuring entity.
b) A certification that the detailed engineering surveys and designs have been conducted according to the prescribed agency standards and detailed engineering outputs are adequate for the procurement at hand.
c) The infrastructure contracts be reviewed and revised to include the provision for arbitration clause so that subsequent contracts which will be entered into by the LGU would comply with the relevant requirements of RA 9184 IRR and related laws.

That whenever medicines are procured the purchase request should bear a certification from the requisitioning officer that the procured items are in compliant with PNDF vol. I current edition.
